title: A collider as a quantum computer
abstract: Scattering processes in high-energy physics are inherently quantum mechanical, yet are typically analyzed at the level of final states, where entanglement appears as a property of the outcome rather than a consequence of the underlying dynamics. We reformulate scattering at the level of the process itself by representing helicity transition matrices as quantum circuits. Once the kinematic configuration and scattering channel are fixed, the problem reduces to a finite-dimensional quantum map, making a circuit description natural. Within this framework, an example of the process $e^+e^-\to μ^+μ^-$ is shown, which decomposes into unitary and non-unitary components, corresponding to coherent mixing and postselection effects. This representation reorganizes the amplitude into distinct operational elements, providing a perspective in which collider processes can be viewed as constrained quantum circuits and their entanglement structure can be understood in terms of the underlying circuit dynamics, opening the door to analyzing their properties using the language of quantum information.
summary: This paper proposes a new framework where high-energy scattering processes are viewed as constrained quantum circuits. By decomposing the scattering T-matrix into quantum gates and Kraus operators, the authors show that the underlying dynamics of particle collisions can be understood through the lens of quantum information theory.
main_problem: The paper seeks to reformulate high-energy particle scattering processes, which are traditionally analyzed via final-state properties, as structured, operational quantum circuits.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrate that the T-matrix for lepton scattering can be decomposed into a two-qubit quantum circuit consisting of Pauli gates, controlled rotations, and CNOT gates, alongside a non-unitary Kraus operator.
method: The study employs Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) to separate coherent mixing from non-unitary effects and Cosine-Sine Decomposition (CSD) to map the resulting unitary matrices onto elementary quantum gates.
model_or_system: The physical system is the helicity degrees of freedom in high-energy lepton scattering, specifically the electron-positron annihilation process (e+e- to mu+mu-) involving photon and Z-boson exchange.
key_observables: Helicity indices, scattering angle (theta), and singular values (sigma_plus, sigma_minus) representing transmission strength.
important_parameters: Weinberg angle (theta_W), electroweak couplings (gL, gR), and the dimensionless momentum parameter (mu) at the 1 TeV scale.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes fixed kinematic configurations and utilizes the high-energy/massless limit where helicity coincides with chirality.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows the dependence of circuit parameters on the scattering angle; Figure 3 illustrates the dynamical correlation between unitary and non-unitary components; Figure 4 displays the tree-level Feynman diagram for the process.
paper_structure: The paper moves from the standard S-matrix description to a T-matrix operator formulation, applies SVD and CSD for matrix decomposition, maps the results to a quantum circuit architecture, and concludes with an analytical demonstration using lepton scattering.
why_interesting: This work provides a novel bridge between high-energy physics and quantum information, offering a framework to study scattering dynamics using the language of quantum gates, Kraus operators, and circuit-based information loss.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
